No, the point of finalize is to register a finalizer (which is better behaved than `__del__`). The first argument is weakrefed, the finalizer is not called until only weakrefs remain. If we have a finalizer method, the reference from that method will keep the node alive (the ref to the callable is not weak), thereby delaying collection indefinitely!

That's a drawback of `maybe_future`. It operates on the value returned by the function, so if the function itself fails to return a value, you're going to throw an exception. This is usually not a problem if you're also doing a `yield` because you expect the exception -- either from the future or from the call, but if you're doing anything more complex you have to handle the other cases.

    yield maybe_future(f(...))

Can fail in either calling `f` or in the `future.result()` called by `yield`. You're fine either way because you expect the exception there.

    maybe_future(f(...)).add_done_callback(..)

If `f` fails, you're not adding any callback. Your callback will never be called.

FWIW: This is happening due to a combination of:
- `and` op having higher precedence over `or` (\[1]), thus `condition and val_on_true` is evaluated first
- which returns, `val_on_true` due to the short-circuit `and` operator (\[2]) (since `condition` is True)
- so then, you evaluate `val_on_true or val_on_false` which due to short-circuiting `or` and Python's string "truthiness" you get the `val_on_false`.

\[1]: https://docs.python.org/2/reference/expressions.html#operator-precedence
\[2]: https://docs.python.org/2/library/stdtypes.html#boolean-operations-and-or-not

Changed back to "always-UTF8" protocol. On Python 2 strings are just bytes - there's no "encoding". On Python 3 ```sys.prefix``` is decoded with ```Py_DecodeLocale``` during initialization. On Windows this function eventually calls ```mbstowcs```. On the other hand, ```sys.getfilesystemencoding()``` is hard-coded to "mbcs", and ```decode('mbcs')``` eventually calls ```MultiByteToWideChar```. If the locale is not changed on the fly, both functions return the same result. As a result, I use ```decode(sys.getfilesystemencoding())``` to get the same result for both Python 2 and Python 3.

This early exit allows us to being able to *skip* the COUNT query since the SELECT query yielded 0 results anyway. 

The reason for why I didn't use the exact filter for the COUNT and the SELECT was because of this test:
https://github.com/peterbe/kinto/blob/51c7bf9ec9eb1f89d7e7d3a015429f2bc525551d/kinto/core/storage/testing.py#L1217-L1218
(there might be more like this!)
In that test it does a `get_all(include_deleted=True, ...)` and it expects the number of records to be different from the total count. 
Isn't that strange? If you `include_deleted=True` why should the total count be always filter out the deleted records??

If that test is wrong, I can rewrite the two queries so that the SELECT and COUNT uses the exact same WHERE clause. Then, if we do that we can do the COUNT first (slightly faster since it's just an integer) and if it's 0 we can skip the SELECT and just return an empty list.

By "failure mode," I mean how does an error manifest itself at runtime? 

If an output (optional or not) is not named, it cannot be used as an input or graph output. What downstream error can arise from that? (I'm not saying there can't be one, it's just not clear to me what it could be.)

By assigning a name to an output that is __not__ computed, errors can arise, depending on the runtime implementation. Since such a name can be used as input to other operators, or as a graph output, uninitialized values may be propagated, which leads to further errors and, in the worst case, crashes. Failing to name an output that is computed could be an indication that the model is incorrectly constructed, which is worth at least a warning during validation, but it doesn't seem to be able to cause corruption of data, and if a model doesn't need the output, then why make it assign a name?

Just asking questions...
